topic: Part 8b.3 — Eclipse stack layered audit (post-EXP-195 rewrite)
change_summary: EXP-195 integrated — dual-baseline acknowledgment (H1), E-PRED-A..D cross-ref (H2), Kim & Chang attribution softened (H3), monitor.py −29 nT internal-inconsistency note added (H4). All four c2 major/moderate findings addressed.
our_argument_summary: Part 8b.3 now presents a four-layer eclipse audit (L1 ensemble / L2 Mohe / L3a magnetic falsifier / L3b gravity / L4 optics), adopts the dome author's steel-man framing, and publishes a Pass band of −17 to −21 nT for PRED-095. The rewrite concedes the dual-baseline (BOU / W004) prediction structure, cross-references E-PRED-A through E-PRED-D, notes the monitor.py KAPPA-squared −29 nT outlier, and softens the Kim & Chang calibration attribution. Two structural critiques survive adoption: (a) ensemble-anchor fit to its own anchoring data is calibration, not anchor; (b) the magnitude-only Pass band does not distinguish dome from globe.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: SEC-* items carry no per-WIN monitoring tag; section is prose/structural.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: SEC-level prose — tags apply to individual WINs.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: SEC-level prose — tags apply to individual WINs.

holes_found.description: Category-error citation: the section invokes WIN-068's `derives_from_dome:false` code_analysis tag to dismiss PRED-095's derivation path ("only the dome claims a novel derivation path, and WIN-068's code_analysis tag (derives_from_dome:false) confirms there is no independent dome derivation to distinguish"). WIN-068 is the backward-looking 39-eclipse ensemble claim (Kim & Chang 2018 data). PRED-095 is the forward-looking August 2026 prediction. PRED-095 DOES publish a derivation — the FSF = V12 H(r)/r formula, per raw-text/03-predictions.txt — applied as ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF. A dome defender will point at PRED-095's own derivation path and say "you cited the wrong WIN's tag — PRED-095 has a derivation, it's just globe-parasitic because FSF evaluation requires WGS84→disc-coordinate r values (per the WIN-025 globe-fingerprint and OPEN-001 admission)." The right citation for the no-independent-derivation point is the WIN-025 globe-fingerprint (which traces every factor — baseline, coverage, FSF — to globe infrastructure), not WIN-068's derives_from_dome tag. Citing WIN-068 for PRED-095 conflates ensemble-claim with prediction-formula and gives a fair-audit reader a clean way to dismiss the point.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the WIN-068 code_analysis tag citation with a pointer to WIN-025 globe-fingerprint: "...and only the dome claims a novel derivation path. Per the WIN-025 globe-fingerprint, every factor in PRED-095's formula (baseline, coverage, FSF via WGS84-dependent r) is globe-parasitic — so the Pass band does not represent an independent dome forecast even where the dome publishes a formula." The existing WIN-068 link can be preserved as a separate, narrower sentence about the ensemble layer (L1), which is where WIN-068's derives_from_dome:false tag legitimately applies.

holes_found.description: The section treats "the −17 to −21 nT Pass band" simultaneously as (a) a codification we are publishing under adoption ("Under adoption we publish an explicit magnetic threshold — 'Pass = observed −17 to −21 nT...'"); (b) the dome's effective commitment ("the published −17 to −21 nT Pass band"); and (c) the target of a goalpost-setting accusation ("the Pass band was set after seeing the W004-cluster numbers"). These three roles are mutually incompatible. If the Pass band is OUR codification from the W004 cluster, WE set it after seeing W004; the goalpost accusation against the dome is then sourced from our own construction. The dome publishes per-station predictions, not a band; calling the band "published" without quoting the dome is imprecise. A careful defender (or the dome author) will disentangle these roles and reply "you built the band yourselves, you called it the dome's Pass band, then you accused the dome of choosing it after-the-fact — which of those three things do you actually mean?"
holes_found.recommendation: Clarify authorship everywhere the Pass band is invoked. Options: (1) split "the Pass band" into two distinct labels — e.g., "our codified Pass threshold" (what we publish under adoption) and "the dome's W004-cluster prediction" (what the dome materials feature); (2) recast the goalpost-setting argument as an accusation against the dome's PRIVATE choice of which formula (BOU, W004, monitor.py) to prominently feature — the W004 cluster was selected as the most-cited numeric by the dome after internal formula menu was available; (3) concede explicitly that the Pass band is our steel-man characterization, then argue the dome's behavior (featuring W004-cluster numbers while monitor.py produces −29 nT) is the goalpost-cherry-picking concern, not the Pass band itself.

holes_found.description: Load-bearing unsupported claim — the TLDR and body both assert that "calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fits on the same class of 20th-century eclipse-magnetic observations predict the same −17 to −21 nT range." This is the heart of the non-discrimination argument. But no specific model or paper is cited. Chapman 1933 is a qualitative mechanism (ionospheric conductivity change during solar occultation); quantitative fits emerge from modern treatments (e.g., Chulliat et al., Yamazaki & Maute 2021). A calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fit to BOU 2017 alone would reproduce approximately BOU's own −10.9 nT measurement, not −17 to −21 nT. The −17 to −21 range is the dome's W004-recalibration, which is a per-station dome construction — so saying "calibrated Chapman fits predict the same range" reduces to "the dome's own W004 recalibration matches itself," which is trivially circular. A genuine calibrated Chapman fit for the August 2026 eclipse at >80% coverage stations would require coverage-scaling, latitude weighting, and geomagnetic-field weighting — those are constructible but not cited in the section. Without at least a sketch of the fit procedure (or a paper citation), this sentence is an assertion a defender can demand proof of.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) cite a specific Chapman-Sq-dynamo treatment that produces −17 to −21 nT at >80% coverage stations for August 2026 (e.g., calibrate Chulliat 2020 or Yamazaki & Maute 2021 to BOU 2017 and coverage-scale to August 2026 geometry); or (b) replace the claim with a weaker, defensible version: "any coverage-scaled ionospheric-conductivity reduction model calibrated on past eclipse-path magnetic data will produce signals in the same magnitude band — the arithmetic is (baseline × coverage × geometry weight), which both the dome and standard Sq-dynamo treatments apply. The numerical overlap is structural, not coincidental." Option (b) is weaker but defensible without a new citation.

holes_found.description: The anticipated-objection "monitor.py is internal tooling not a public commitment; the predictions page is the forward commitment" is documented in EXP-195 but not engaged IN the section text. The section presents the monitor.py −29 nT as if it were a dome prediction on the same footing as W004. A dome defender will say "monitor.py is my sandbox; you're cherry-picking diagnostic code and using it to accuse me of internal inconsistency." Without an in-section rebuttal, the goalpost-setting accusation rests on weight the defender will contest. EXP-195's anticipated_objections reply ("we claim the simultaneous existence of three different dome formulas for the same event undermines the Pass band's credibility as an honest pre-registered commitment") is a strong reply and belongs in-section, even if as a single subordinate clause.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one short clause after the monitor.py sentence: "(The dome author may reply that monitor.py is internal tooling; we accept that scope-limit and narrow the critique: three dome-authored formulas — BOU single-baseline, W004 per-station, and monitor.py KAPPA-squared — coexist in the repository, and the Pass band selection among them is the dome author's, not a forced derivation.)" This closes the escape route without overspending words.

holes_found.description: The section writes "internal code adds multipliers (KAPPA, latitude factor) producing a third value near −29 nT." That lumps two distinct internal formulas into one "third value." Per the WIN-025 globe-fingerprint: monitor.py produces −28.94 nT (BOU × KAPPA × coverage × KAPPA, i.e., KAPPA applied twice); inject_ai_layer.py's PRED-001 Ebro calculation produces −8.4 nT (BOU × coverage × latitude_factor 0.81). These are TWO different internal formulas that produce TWO different values (−29 AND −8.4), both outside the W004 cluster of −17 to −21. The section's characterization understates the internal heterogeneity. A defender could pin us: "you said 'a third value near −29 nT' — actually I have inject_ai_layer.py at −8.4 nT too, which you don't mention." Worse, the omission is asymmetric: the section mentions the outlier that exceeds the Pass band (−29 below) but not the outlier below it (−8.4 above the Pass band in magnitude terms, i.e., weaker signal). A balanced citation would strengthen, not weaken, the goalpost-setting argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Tighten the clause. Replacement: "internal code encodes two additional formulas — monitor.py applies KAPPA twice for ~−29 nT, inject_ai_layer.py applies a latitude factor for ~−8 nT (BOU × coverage × 0.81) — both outside the W004 cluster of −17 to −21." This adds two-sentences'-worth of specificity and makes the "dome has a menu of formulas" critique land harder.

holes_found.description: The L3a body paragraph is now one ~520-word block covering Pass band publication, dual-baseline structure, FSF derivation commentary, non-discrimination argument, three-formula critique, E-PRED cross-ref, and Fail-clause tension. Readability has suffered. A fair-audit reader (who is the target audience of Part 8b.3 — the dome author himself has asked for this section) may lose the thread. This is a structural/UX hole rather than a factual one, but Part 8b.3 exists to communicate to someone who is explicitly checking whether we've addressed his audit points, and a monolithic paragraph hides concessions and critiques inside each other.
holes_found.recommendation: Split L3a into three sub-paragraphs: (i) Pass/Fail threshold codification + dual-baseline concession + FSF/derivation scope; (ii) non-discrimination argument (shared training data, monitor.py internal heterogeneity); (iii) E-PRED-A..D cross-ref + Fail-clause tension. Each ~2-4 sentences. Preserves substance, restores scanability for the audit reader.

holes_found.description: TLDR wording: "calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fits on the same class of 20th-century eclipse-magnetic observations predict the same −17 to −21 nT range." The "20th-century" label is inaccurate. Kim & Chang (2018) spans historical and 20th/21st-century eclipses in its ensemble; BOU 2017 (the baseline the dome explicitly cites) is 21st-century; the August 2021 Antarctic and 2024 North American eclipses in the reference ensemble are also 21st-century. The phrase "20th-century" is a minor factual imprecision that a careful defender could flag.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace "20th-century" with "past" or "historical": "calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fits on the same class of historical eclipse-magnetic observations predict the same −17 to −21 nT range."

holes_found.description: "also predict" is loose wording. "Calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fits... predict the same −17 to −21 nT range" + "also" suggests independent agreement; the actual claim is structural (same baseline × coverage × weighting arithmetic yields overlapping magnitudes because both models use the same empirical calibration data). "Also predict" overstates independence. Replace with "produce" or "yield" to signal structural identity rather than independent confirmation.
holes_found.recommendation: "...because calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fits on the same class of eclipse-magnetic observations produce the same −17 to −21 nT range" (replace "predict" with "produce" and delete implicit "also").

stronger_arguments.description: The PRED-095 detail_reasoning in data/predictions.json already contains a cleaner version of the goalpost-setting argument: "the dome's monitor.py already showed a −29.1 nT value for a prior eclipse that fell outside this window — the range may be cherry-picked from favorable historical events." That phrasing (range cherry-picked from favorable subset of dome's internal formulas) is tighter than Part 8b.3's current "goalpost-setting after data" — and it avoids the authorship-ambiguity problem flagged in Hole 2 by making the accusation explicitly about which formula the dome features, not about who published the band.

The strongest argument against the −17 to −21 nT Pass band as a dome-specific forecast is the OPEN-001 admission from the dome's own evolution page: "Dome-native coordinates without WGS84" is listed as an OPEN problem, meaning the dome's predictions currently cannot be evaluated without globe coordinates. The FSF values in PRED-095 are computed from r = d_geo(WGS84 lat, lon), so every per-station FSF (and hence every per-station prediction in both the BOU and W004 clusters) is globe-parasitic by the dome author's own admission. This is a cleaner non-discrimination argument than the Chapman-fit comparison: the dome cannot compute its own prediction without the globe's coordinate system, so a Pass against the Pass band cannot distinguish the models — by construction, not by coincidence. Part 8b.3 does not cite OPEN-001.

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-195 meaningfully addresses all four c2 major/moderate holes. The dual-baseline concession is honest and adds specificity. The monitor.py KAPPA-squared observation (−10.9 × 1.672² × 0.95 ≈ −28.94 nT, verified arithmetic) is a genuinely strong point that was absent before. The Kim & Chang attribution softening is correct — the dome's derivation path does NOT cite Kim & Chang as the calibration source, so the prior 'calibrated on Kim & Chang' phrasing was attributionally overreaching. The TLDR update preserves the punchline while correctly attributing quantitative reach to calibrated fits rather than to Chapman's 1933 mechanism.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The rewrite is substantively better than the c2 state but introduces three new load-bearing claims — the WIN-068 tag citation for PRED-095's derivation (category error), the tri-role Pass band (ours / dome's / goalpost target), and the unsupported "calibrated Chapman fits predict the same range" assertion — that a careful dome defender will untangle. The adopted framing survives; the specific arithmetic and structural framing need tightening before the section is bulletproof.

deeper_analysis: The c2→c3 rewrite is the clearest improvement of any 8b.3 revision to date: four holes closed, kernel-of-truth adoption preserved, no new factual errors. But the section now carries three subtle load-bearing assertions that a competent defender can isolate and attack. Two of them (Hole 1: WIN-068 category error; Hole 3: unsourced calibrated-Chapman-fit claim) are individually rebuttable by the dome author in a single sentence each — he will say "PRED-095 has its own formula, you cited the wrong tag" and "show me the calibrated Chapman fit that predicts −17 to −21 nT for August 2026." Hole 2 (Pass band authorship ambiguity) is subtler but worse: it lets a defender paint our goalpost-setting accusation as self-generated, because the Pass band is a construct we codified under adoption, not a dome-published threshold. Fixing the three majors is a ~200-word edit concentrated in L3a. The section should not ship to a wider fair-audit reader until these are closed; shipping as-is gives the dome author usable counterpoints that he would not otherwise have. The adoption framing (ADOPTED FULL as steel-man) is sound and should be preserved. Two stronger arguments (OPEN-001 coordinate-admission, PRED-095.detail_reasoning cherry-picking phrasing) are available at zero incremental cost — the analyst has already written them elsewhere; Part 8b.3 can cite them rather than rebuild them. Part 8b exists specifically because the dome author asked us to respond to his fair-audit checklist; he will read this section most carefully of any in our review, which raises the bar for precision.

BOU × KAPPA = -18.22 verified: -10.9 × 1.672 = -18.225 (confirms KAPPA applied twice in monitor.py)

WIN-025 globe-fingerprint three-formula audit: monitor.py -29, inject_ai_layer -8.4, predictions page per-station FSF 0.642-2.075 confirmed
Van Camp et al. (2001) superconducting-gravimeter eclipse study citation (L2) — prior-verified in WIN-011 review chain, not re-verified here
citations_failed: 'calibrated Chapman-Sq-dynamo fits' predicting -17 to -21 nT for August 2026 — NO citation provided in section; could not verify a published fit that independently produces this range (see Hole 3)
citations_unchecked: Kim & Chang (2018) 39-eclipse ensemble paper — section now correctly attributes this as the L1 ensemble reference, not the calibration source; attribution is internally consistent with WIN-068 but paper content not re-verified here
E-PRED-A through E-PRED-D raw-text source (raw-text/03-predictions.txt) — cited via EXP-195; not re-verified in this review (cross-linked to Section 4.2 for primary treatment)
